arctic.title: The Arctic: Our Planet's Canary
arctic.heroSubtitle: A data-driven story of sea-ice decline by Lukas Kreibig
arctic.heroLine1: The Arctic Is Warming Nearly Four Times Faster Than the Global Average
arctic.heroLine2: Sea-Ice Melt Is Now Everyday Reality
arctic.heroLine3: Scientists forecast ice-free Arctic summers before 2050
arctic.heroLine4: The Arctic is the planet's canary.

loading.title: Preparing the map and satellite layers
loading.detail: Data, imagery, and the Arctic flyover are loaded before the story begins.

alt.pushingSled: A person pushing a sled across the ice
alt.fisherboat: A fishing boat in the harbour
alt.polarBear: A polar bear on fragile sea ice

geographic.title: How the Ice Is Vanishing
geographic.intro: In the Arctic, sea ice is not just scenery. For many places it is route, workplace, and risk. What changes when that season becomes shorter and harder to trust?

greenland.title: Greenland
greenland.description: The world's largest island is mostly ice sheet. Along the rocky coast, deep fjords cut inland and create sheltered places where people have lived with the Arctic sea for millennia.

uummannaqBay.title: Uummannaq Bay
uummannaqBay.description: 600 kilometers north of the Arctic Circle, this bay opens into one of Greenland's most dramatic fjord systems. In winter, the sea itself becomes the road.

uummannaqIsland.title: Uummannaq Island
uummannaqIsland.description: About 1,300 people live here beneath a distinctive heart-shaped mountain. In Uummannaq, ice is not scenery. It is mobility, timing, and safety.
uummannaqIsland.changing: The question is how that reliability is changing.

heartOfSeal.title: Heart of a Seal
heartOfSeal.description: Uummannaq takes its name from the mountain known as the Heart of a Seal. Beneath it, the town sits between harbor, fjord, and winter routes.

winterMonths.description: When the fjord freezes, the map changes. Routes that do not exist in summer become possible by sled, snowmobile, or on foot, but only while the ice can be trusted.

fishing.description: Fishing still shapes Uummannaq. But the story is not only climate loss: ice conditions, harbor access, markets, boats, and deliberate ice breakup all affect daily life.

voices.title: Voices from Uummannaq
voices.description: In the Life on Thin Ice study, residents describe how winter ice, travel, hunting, and fishing are changing. Their observations are where this measurement begins.
voices.learnMoreLink: Find out more about the study "Life on thin ice"
voices.learnMoreContent: The paper "Life on thin ice: Insights from Uummannaq, Greenland for connecting climate science with Arctic communities" connects fieldwork in Uummannaq Bay with a review of sea-ice research. Interviews and a focus group show why predictable winter ice matters for hunting, fishing, and travel, and why coastal ice thickness is underrepresented in mainstream research. Newer work on co-production adds an important caution: local ice change is not a single-cause story. Fishing, harbor access, deliberate ice breakup, modernization, and local knowledge all shape how ice is used and experienced.

quotes.iceDisappears:
When I was a child,
the ice was gone in June or July.
Now it is gone in April or May.
quotes.unstableClimate:
The most important change is the less stable weather.
The wind has become stronger
and harder to predict.
quotes.motorsledge:
Many hunters now prefer snowmobiles to dog sleds.
The winters are shorter,
and the catch has to move faster.
quotes.source: from the "Life on Thin Ice" study

measurement.title: Only a Feeling?
measurement.intro: But how do you test an observation like that? From above. For years, ESA's Sentinel-2 satellites have passed over Greenland and captured the fjord: image by image, winter by winter, until they add up to a comparable record.
measurement.step1: First, the view from above: this is Uummannaq as the satellite sees it.
measurement.step2: Now the raw winter image. Scenes like this are the basis for the measurement.
measurement.step3: Since 2017, ESA's Sentinel programme has provided regular images. Thousands of scenes have to become one consistent record.
measurement.step4: A computer-vision pipeline reads each image. It masks clouds and land, then separates ice from open water.
measurement.step5: The overlay is not the conclusion. It is the audit trail between satellite image, model, and chart.
measurement.learnMoreLink: Learn More about the Computer Vision Pipeline
measurement.learnMoreTitle: Computer Vision Pipeline for Sea Ice Detection
measurement.learnMoreContent:
High-resolution Sentinel-2 images (all 13 bands) are streamed from AWS and pass through a four-stage segmentation pipeline:

• Black-border filter removes corrupt edge pixels.
• Cloud mask: a U-Net model (CloudSEN12+) flags thin clouds and shadows.
• Land mask: a static coastline raster excludes rock and tundra.
• Ice/Water mask: spectral indices separate
  – solid ice  NDSI > 0.52
  – light / wet ice 0.31–0.52
  – open water NDWI > 0.25.

Masks are merged into a colour overlay (yellow = solid ice, cyan = light ice, blue = water). Statistics for every tile are stored in a CSV file for later analysis.

pixelInspector.title: From Image to Evidence
pixelInspector.raw: The satellite image is still a photograph of light. It has texture, shadows, snow, water, and uncertainty.
pixelInspector.mask: The overlay turns that image into classes. It does not replace judgment; it makes the judgment visible.
pixelInspector.classification: Each classified pixel is small. Together, thousands of them become an estimate of ice cover for that scene.
pixelInspector.outputTitle: Then It Becomes a Time Series
pixelInspector.output: Only after the same procedure is repeated across many scenes can one winter be compared with another.

memoryMeasurement.title: Memory Meets Measurement
memoryMeasurement.build: Watch one season fill in: the ice forms in February, holds through spring, then breaks apart by June. Each mark is one day, one satellite scene.
memoryMeasurement.dayTitle: One scene becomes one daily value
memoryMeasurement.day: This day is the bridge between the satellite image and the table: the pipeline estimates how much of the water surface is covered by ice.
memoryMeasurement.season: The sequence shows how a winter builds, holds, and breaks apart.
memoryMeasurement.intro: Now all measured seasons are in view. Each row is one winter in Uummannaq; each small mark is one day.
memoryMeasurement.early: The early years are the comparison point, not a perfect past.
memoryMeasurement.late: The later years carry the summary value. It is a guide to the shift, not the whole explanation.
memoryMeasurement.latest: One year alone is never the story. It matters because it belongs to the pattern around it.
memoryMeasurement.caveat: Clouds, missing scenes, and local use remain part of the uncertainty. Weather, fishing economics, harbor access, deliberate ice breakup, and local knowledge all shape what ice means in practice.

visualProof.title: Season by Season
visualProof.description: Each small chart shows one Uummannaq ice season from February to June. The point is not one dramatic year, but the rhythm across years.
visualProof.numbersDescription: Across the measured years, the later seasons sit lower in the same calendar window.
visualProof.trend: Less ice in the measured season
visualProof.conclusion: The satellite record and local memory point the same way: earlier breakup, while leaving room for local causes and year-to-year variability.

newAbnormal.title: When "Normal" Shifts
newAbnormal.description: To make the shift legible, we compare the first four measured seasons with the later measured years.
newAbnormal.earlyPeriod: The blue line shows the 2017-2020 mean. The light band shows how much those years varied.
newAbnormal.livingOutside: The red line shows the 2021-2025 mean. In this comparison, the later years sit lower across much of the season.
newAbnormal.consequence: is the estimate for this comparison window.
newAbnormal.result: That does not explain every local decision, but it does show that the physical season has shifted.

trajectory.title: A Direction, Not a Fate
trajectory.rate: The yearly record slopes downward. Treat the line as evidence of direction, not as a fixed date for the future.

recall.title: Back to the Opening Memory
recall.description: The story began with a memory that spans a lifetime: as a child, the ice held into June or July. Nine years of satellite data is far too short to test a childhood recollection. But it can ask the same question in numbers. Each dot is the day one Uummannaq winter's ice broke up.

shift.title: Earlier, on Average
shift.description: Across the measured years, breakup moved from a late-May average toward mid-May, about eleven days earlier. It is a short, nine-year window, so read it as a direction, not a fixed date.

unpredictable.title: And Harder to Predict
unpredictable.description: The bigger change is the swing: recent winters break up as early as late April or as late as June. That matches the other thing residents stressed: the season has become less stable and harder to trust. The satellite and the people on the ice describe one change from two sides: one measures the area, the other lives the risk.

future.trend: A shorter period of reliable ice changes the balance between ice roads, boats, harbor work, and safety decisions.
future.memory: For children in Uummannaq, ice is both inherited memory and a changing practical problem.
future.traditions: Dog teams, snowmobiles, boats, and local judgment now share a more unstable season.

toArctic.title: From Uummannaq to the Arctic
toArctic.description: Uummannaq shows one local shift. The Arctic-wide record gives the scale: winter maximums, summer minimums, old ice, and rapid regional warming.
toArctic.learnMoreTitle: Methodology & Data Sources – Arctic Sea-Ice Dashboard
toArctic.learnMoreContent:
The dashboard combines peer-reviewed datasets and refreshes them daily via an automated Python pipeline.

Data sources
NASA GISS – temperature anomalies: https://data.giss.nasa.gov/gistemp/
NOAA – daily Arctic sea-ice extent: https://nsidc.org/sea-ice-today
Our World in Data – global CO₂ emissions: https://ourworldindata.org/co2-and-greenhouse-gas-emissions

Processing steps
1. A GitHub Actions workflow triggers an update every night.
2. Latest CSV / JSON files are downloaded, validated and reshaped with Pandas.
3. Z-scores standardise temperature, ice extent and CO₂ to a common scale.
4. The cleaned dataset is written to data.json and served by FastAPI to the Next.js frontend.

arcticGlobe.title: What Summer Leaves Behind
arcticGlobe.description: At the end of each summer the sea ice pulls back to its yearly minimum. Seen from above, decade by decade, the loss is unmistakable: the September minimum is shrinking by about 13 percent per decade.
arcticGlobe.attribution: Sea ice: NSIDC Sea Ice Index
arcticGlobe.aria: Animated map: Arctic September sea ice shown on a 3-D globe. From 1980 to 2024 the ice cap visibly retreats; the September minimum is shrinking by roughly 13 percent per decade. Source: NSIDC Sea Ice Index.

seasonal.title: The Arctic Seasonal Record
seasonal.description: Each line traces one year of Arctic sea-ice extent, from autumn minimum to spring maximum and back again. Blue lines show older years, red lines show recent years.

early.title: 1979–2000: The Cold(er) Half
early.description: The first half of the record sits visibly higher. The ice had more room to recover through the year.

late.title: 2000 onward: The Recent Half
late.description: In the more recent years, the lines sit lower through much of the season.

current.title: Latest Available Year {{year}}
current.description: Finally, isolate {{year}} and compare it with the rest of the record.

breaking.title: Below the Earlier Range
breaking.description: Recent years often sit below the older envelope, especially around the winter maximum and late-summer minimum.

decades.title: Six Decades, One Direction
decades.description: Each colored band represents a decade of Arctic ice measurements. Watch them appear one by one and notice the unmistakable staircase downward. We open in the 1980s, the last decade before the losses dive below zero.

1980s.title: The 1980s: The Old Normal
1980s.description: This soft blue band is our starting point. Late-summer values still hovered around zero million km², close to the historical average and a world many remember as 'normal.'

1990s.title: The 1990s: First Cracks
1990s.description: Each new decade settles slightly lower than the last. The changes seem gradual, but they're building momentum.

2000s.title: The 2000s: A Lower Baseline
2000s.description: For the first time, the curve spends much of the season below the older baseline. The shift is no longer subtle.

2010s.title: The 2010s: Acceleration
2010s.description: The decline steepens. Values around -0.6 million km² become common, territory that was unthinkable just decades earlier.

2020s.title: The 2020s: A Lower Range
2020s.description: The current decade sits near the bottom of the stack. The gap from the old baseline is especially clear late in the melt season.
2020s.acceleration: The data shows no short-term return to the older range.

bridge.decades: Each new decade stepped lower. But how did single years behave inside those stair-steps?

annual.title: Below the Average
annual.description: Each bar shows how much above (blue) or below (red) the long-term average that year finished. Think of zero as the "normal" line.
annual.analysis: The 1980s and 90s still moved around the average. From the 2000s onward, negative years become the rule rather than the exception.
annual.conclusion: The single-year view tells the same story: the baseline has moved lower.

drivers.intro: What is pushing the ice away? We have seen declining coverage and strong anomalies. Next, we put CO₂, global temperature, and Arctic temperature on the same scale.

connections.title: The Warming Signal
connections.description: Three lines, one story: global CO₂ emissions (green), global temperature (blue), and Arctic temperature (red) have climbed for decades.
connections.causation: The physical link is well established: more greenhouse gases trap more heat. In the Arctic, that warming shows up especially strongly.
connections.conclusion: The Arctic is an early warning system because regional change there connects back to weather, oceans, and climate elsewhere.

zscore.title: Apples to Apples
zscore.description: How do you compare CO₂ emissions (measured in tonnes) with temperature (degrees) and ice extent (square kilometers)?
zscore.method: By converting each series to the same kind of distance from its own historical average. Different units become comparable without pretending they are the same.

mirror.title: The Mirror Image
mirror.description: When we flip the ice-loss data upside down, the pattern becomes easier to compare: rising CO₂ and temperature move against falling ice.
mirror.conclusion: Three different measurements do not become the same thing. They become readable on the same scale.

2024.title: {{year}}: The Latest Comparison
2024.description: In the latest available year, {{year}}, the Arctic temperature anomaly sits well above the global mean.
2024.warning: The red bar is more than a number. It shows how unevenly warming is distributed.
2024.conclusion: The comparison is a reminder that global averages can hide sharp regional change.

dataProcessing.title: 2. What data do we process & why?
dataProcessing.serverLogsDetails: (IP address, timestamp, URL, referrer, user agent)
dataProcessing.browserEnd: Until browser end or max. 1 year
dataProcessing.contractCompletion: Until completion of process + legal retention

thirdParty.title: 3. Third-party Services Used
thirdParty.mapbox: Mapbox Inc. (USA) is used for map displays. The IP address is only transmitted in shortened form. The legal basis is our legitimate interest in an appealing presentation (Art. 6 Para. 1 lit. f GDPR).

rights.title: 4. Your Rights
rights.complaint: Complaint to a supervisory authority – Art. 77 GDPR

changes.title: 5. Changes to this Statement
changes.description: We reserve the right to adapt this privacy policy to reflect changes in legal requirements or new services. Please check the current status regularly.

chatbot.greeting: Greetings, traveller! I'm Knud Rasmussen, Danish-Greenlandic explorer and collector of Inuit legends. Ask me anything about the Greenlandic folktales I recorded in Eskimo Folk-Tales. Behind the scenes, modern technology helps me recall these stories clearly, blending the spirit of my journeys with today's AI.
chatbot.error: An error occurred. The spirits have gone silent. Please try again later.
chatbot.description: This AI chatbot is trained on the 'Eskimo Folk-Tales', a unique collection of Greenlandic legends and myths gathered by Danish-Greenlandic polar explorer Knud Rasmussen (1879-1933) during his legendary Thule Expeditions.
chatbot.aboutKnud: Known as the 'Father of Eskimology,' Knud Rasmussen conducted groundbreaking expeditions across the Arctic from 1912-1924. Fluent in Greenlandic, he collected over 20,000 pages of Inuit oral traditions and stories of spirits, shamans, hunting, and Arctic life.
chatbot.folkTales: The 'Eskimo Folk-Tales' contain hundreds of stories: from Tulugaq the great raven who brought light to the world, to Sedna the sea goddess. These tales offer unique insights into the spiritual world of the Inuit.
chatbot.aiNote: Greetings, traveller. Ask me about specific legends, characters, or the meaning behind Arctic myths!

closing.lead: Listening is not the same as measuring.
closing.body: The satellites count the ice. The people of Uummannaq live with what its absence takes: routes, timing, trust, a way of being on the sea. The record here is nine years; the memory is a lifetime. Both point the same way.
closing.question: The future is unwritten. What we make of the measurement and the memory is the open question.

beta.title: Beta Preview
beta.description: This interactive Arctic story is still under development. You might encounter rough edges, visual hiccups or missing features while I smooth things out.
beta.feedback: I'd love your feedback along the way!

ariaSummaries.allYearsSeason: Small-multiple charts of daily ice fraction for each Uummannaq season from 2017; later seasons sit lower in the same February-to-June window.
ariaSummaries.memoryMeasurement: Calendar heatmap of measured ice days for each Uummannaq winter, February to June; later winters show fewer ice days.
ariaSummaries.breakupTiming: Scatter of the day each Uummannaq winter's ice broke up, 2017 to 2025; breakup is about eleven days earlier on average and far more variable.
ariaSummaries.earlyLateSeason: Mean ice fraction for 2017 to 2020 (blue) overlaid on 2021 to 2025 (red) across the season; the later period sits about 11.9 percent lower.
ariaSummaries.seasonal: Line chart of Arctic sea-ice extent by year since 1979; recent years (red) sit below older years (blue), especially at the winter maximum and late-summer minimum.
ariaSummaries.dailyAnomaly: Arctic sea-ice anomaly by decade; each decade steps lower, from near the average in the 1980s to well below it in the 2020s.
ariaSummaries.annualAnomaly: Yearly Arctic sea-ice anomaly bars; from the 2000s onward most years fall below the long-term average.
ariaSummaries.multiLine: Atmospheric CO2, global temperature, and Arctic temperature since the 1980s all rise, with Arctic temperature rising fastest.
ariaSummaries.zScore: CO2, temperature, and sea ice standardized to one scale; rising CO2 and temperature move opposite to falling sea ice.
ariaSummaries.bar2024: Bar chart comparing the Arctic and global temperature anomaly in the latest year; the Arctic anomaly is far larger.

allYearsSeason.title: Daily ice fraction in every season
allYearsSeason.source: Uummannaq data through latest processed season
allYearsSeason.emptyBody: The story can continue, but this chart needs the /uummannaq response.

memoryMeasurement.title: Measured ice days from February to June
memoryMeasurement.subtitle: Each row is one Uummannaq season. Dark cells are open water; blue cells are ice.
memoryMeasurement.axisLabel: February to June, day by day
memoryMeasurement.metricDetail: Mean change in the comparison window
memoryMeasurement.emptyBody: The memory-vs-measurement view needs daily fjord ice fractions.
memoryMeasurement.caveatBody: The chart measures ice cover. It does not measure every reason people can or cannot use the ice.

pixelInspector.title: How a scene becomes a number
pixelInspector.body: The chart later shows one value per day. This is the step that creates it: raw pixels become classes, then a scene-level ice fraction.
pixelInspector.rawAlt: Raw Sentinel-2 winter scene near Uummannaq
pixelInspector.calloutRaw: At this stage it is still an image. Bright snow, ice, clouds, and shadows can look similar.
pixelInspector.calloutMask: The colored overlay is the model's reading of the same image.
pixelInspector.calloutOutput: Many daily values like this become the record shown in the next table.
pixelInspector.metricDetail: The same calculation is repeated for every usable scene.

ice.title: solid ice
ice.body: Bright, continuous surfaces are counted as ice when the spectral signal is consistent.

thinIce.title: thin or wet ice
thinIce.body: Mixed surfaces sit between ice and water and carry more uncertainty.

water.title: open water
water.body: Dark areas are read as water after land and cloud masks are removed.

dailyAnomaly.title: Arctic sea-ice anomaly by decade
dailyAnomaly.emptyBody: The chart needs the decadal anomaly series from /data.

earlyLateSeason.bandHint: Bands show the middle spread of each period.
earlyLateSeason.emptyBody: The comparison needs seasonal summary rows from /uummannaq.

seasonal.title: Arctic sea-ice extent
seasonal.emptyBody: The chart needs daily Arctic sea-ice extent from /data.

all.title: How to read it
all.body: Each line is one year. The whole bundle is the seasonal rhythm.

both.title: How to read it
both.body: Older and newer years stay visible so the shift can be compared.

first.title: Earlier years
first.body: The earlier half sits higher through much of the year.

early.title: Earlier years
early.body: The earlier half sits higher through much of the year.

second.title: Recent years
second.body: The recent half clusters lower, especially near the seasonal extremes.

late.title: Recent years
late.body: The recent half clusters lower, especially near the seasonal extremes.

current.title: Latest year
current.body: One year matters most when it is read against the full record.

annualAnomaly.title: Yearly Arctic sea-ice anomaly
annualAnomaly.emptyBody: The chart needs yearly anomaly rows from /data.

multiLine.title: CO₂, global temperature, Arctic temperature
multiLine.emptyBody: The chart needs annual temperature and CO₂ rows from /data.

zScore.title: CO₂, temperature, sea ice on one scale
zScore.emptyBody: The chart needs annual z-score fields from /data.
zScore.calloutRawBody: Z-scores show how far each series sits from its own average.
zScore.calloutInvertedBody: Flipping sea ice makes the relationship easier to see: more heat, less ice.

bar2024.title: Arctic and global temperature anomaly in {{year}}
bar2024.noData: No usable Arctic/global temperature data found.

meanIceFraction.title: Yearly mean ice fraction
meanIceFraction.emptyBody: The trend needs yearly mean ice fractions from /uummannaq.
meanIceFraction.calloutBody: The trend describes the measured record. It is not a fixed forecast.

breakupTiming.title: When the ice broke up, year by year
